---
title: "Client Report - Star Wars for Dummies"
subtitle: "Unit 5 FullProject"
author: "Sydney Ahlstrom Sanders"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: body
    number-sections: false
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-copy: hover
    code-tools:
        source: false
        toggle: true
        caption: See code
execute: 
  warning: false
    
---

```{python}
import polars as pl 
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *
# add the additional libraries you need to import for ML here
import requests
import io
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, classification_report

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
```


```{python}
# import your data here using polars and the URL
sw_url = "https://byuidatascience.github.io/DS250-Course-Polars/Data/StarWars_with_emails.csv"

response = requests.get(sw_url)
content = response.content.decode('latin1')

start_index = content.find("RespondentID")
clean_content = content[start_index:]

sw = pl.read_csv(io.BytesIO(clean_content.encode('latin1')), truncate_ragged_lines=True)
print(sw.columns)
```

<!-- Rather than a simple write up like we have been doing so far, this project requires a more structured approach. You will need to show you have completed the tasks, but will structure the report in a way that is more like a client report. The report will be structured as follows:

 - Executive Summary: This is a high level overview of the analysis, including the problem statement, the data used, and the main findings. It should be concise and to the point, summarizing the key insights/recommendations without going into too much detail. These are usually bullet points, no graphs. PLEASE EXPLICITLY STATE IN THIS SECTION WHETHER YOU DID THE STRETCH OR NOT! 
 - Methods: this is a high level overview of the methods used in the analysis. This should include an explanation of data preparation steps, especially how you dealt with each categorical variable and why. Showing the resulting or original distribution of variables may be helpful. Describe how you handled missing values and outliers. How did/might your decisions impact results?Explain any feature engineering steps you took. This is also where you will put the graphs you recreated to show you validated the dataset and that it matches the article.
 - Results: this is a more in-depth explanation of results. A classification report and feature importance graphs belong here.
 - Conclusion: this is where you will discuss the implication of the results, including any limitations of the analysis, and recommendations for actions/future work. It may feel repetitive to the executive summary, but it should be more detailed and include any additional insights you gained from the analysis. -->

## Executive Summary

_The objective of this analysis was to process survey data from FiveThirtyEight regarding Star Wars fans to help enable targeted marketing and predictive modeling. I was asked to clean the dataset, extract any meaningful information, and build a model to predict high-income respondents. The results confirm that Star Wars survey data is a reliable source for behavioral insights. My recommendations would be to use the extracted names for Star Wars Day promotions, and to focus high income marketing efforts on the demographics that are identified in the feature importance results with the highest scores._

_Data Preparation: I successfully cleaned the "messy" survey dataset, extracting respondent names from email addresses and mapping categorical preferences to numeric scales for easier understanding._

_Machine Learning: I developed a Random Forest model to predict if a respondent earns at least $50,000 annually. The model achieved an accuracy of approximately 59.88%, identifying age and level of education as the most significant predictors of income (followed by their opinion on Jar Jar Binks)._

_STRETCH CHALLENGE: Yes, the stretch challenge was completed by recreating two of the original chart visualizations from the article and showing that the data used here matches the original findings, validating the dataset._

## Methods

### Task 1
_The raw data contained metadata before the headers, which I bypassed using find RespondentID and io.BytesIO. To assist the marketing team, I used Regex to parse the email column into first name, last name, and a standardized lowercase email domain. As required, there is a table below showing the number of times each email domain is used among the respondents._

```{python}
sw_cleaned = sw.with_columns(
    name_part = pl.col("email").str.extract(r"^([^@]+)"),
    email_domain = pl.col("email").str.extract(r"@([^.]+)").str.to_lowercase().str.strip_chars()
).with_columns(
    # FIRST NAME
    first_name = pl.when(pl.col("name_part").str.contains(r"\."))
      .then(pl.col("name_part").str.extract(r"^([^.]+)"))
      .when(pl.col("name_part").str.contains(r"_"))
      .then(pl.col("name_part").str.extract(r"_([a-zA-Z]+)"))
      .otherwise(pl.col("name_part").str.extract(r"([a-zA-Z]+)$")),
                
    # LAST NAME
    last_name = pl.when(pl.col("name_part").str.contains(r"\."))
      .then(pl.col("name_part").str.extract(r"\.([^@]+)"))
      .when(pl.col("name_part").str.contains(r"_"))
      .then(pl.col("name_part").str.extract(r"^([a-zA-Z]+)_"))
      .otherwise(pl.col("name_part").str.extract(r"^([a-zA-Z]+)"))
)

# Verify
print("Final Name Extraction")
print(sw_cleaned.select(["email", "first_name", "last_name", "email_domain"]).head(5))
```

```{python}
domain_counts = (
    sw_cleaned
    .group_by("email_domain")
    .len()
    .sort("len", descending=True)
)

print("Survey Respondents by Email Domain")
print(domain_counts)
```

### Task 2
_Per the client's request, I filtered the dataset to the 835 respondents who had seen at least one film by checking for non-null values across the episode columns. To prepare for machine learning, I converted categorical ranges (Age, Education, Income, and Favorability) into numeric scales from 1-5 other than age which is 1-4._

```{python}
sw_filtered = sw_cleaned.filter(
    pl.any_horizontal(pl.col("^seen_episode_.*$").is_not_null())
)

print(f"Filtered rows: {sw_filtered.height}")
```

```{python}
# 1. Age Mapping
age_map = {
    "18-29": 1,
    "30-44": 2,
    "45-60": 3,
    "> 60": 4
}

# 2. Education Mapping
edu_map = {
    "Less than high school degree": 1,
    "High school degree": 2,
    "Some college or Associate degree": 3,
    "Bachelor degree": 4,
    "Graduate degree": 5
}

# 3. Income Mapping
income_map = {
    "$0 - $24,999": 1,
    "$25,000 - $49,999": 2,
    "$50,000 - $99,999": 3,
    "$100,000 - $149,999": 4,
    "$150,000+": 5
}

# 4. Favorability Mapping
fav_map = {
    "Very unfavorably": 1,
    "Somewhat unfavorably": 2,
    "Neither favorably nor unfavorably (neutral)": 3,
    "Unfamiliar (please leave blank)": 3, # Treating unfamiliar as neutral
    "Somewhat favorably": 4,
    "Very favorably": 5
}

# Apply mappings and remove original columns
sw_ml = sw_filtered.with_columns([
    pl.col("age").replace_strict(age_map, default=None).cast(pl.Int64).alias("age_num"),
    pl.col("education").replace_strict(edu_map, default=None).cast(pl.Int64).alias("edu_num"),
    pl.col("household_income").replace_strict(income_map, default=None).cast(pl.Int64).alias("income_num"),
    
    pl.col(["han_solo", "luke_skywalker", "princess_leia_organa", "anakin_skywalker", 
            "obi_wan_kenobi", "emperor_palpatine", "darth_vader", "lando_calrissian", 
            "boba_fett", "C_3PO", "R2_D2", "jar_jar_binks", "padme_amidala", "yoda"])
      .replace_strict(fav_map, default=None).cast(pl.Int64)
]).drop(["age", "education", "household_income"])

# Preview
print(sw_ml.select(["age_num", "edu_num", "income_num", "yoda", "han_solo"]).head())
```

```{python}
# Validation table to show the mapping worked
validation_table = (
    sw_ml.select([
        "first_name", 
        "age_num", 
        "edu_num", 
        "income_num", 
        "han_solo", 
        "yoda"
    ]).head(10)
)

print("Final Data Excerpt")
print(validation_table)
```

### Task 3
_I finalized the data by dropping identifiers (Name, Email, RespondentID) and applying One-Hot Encoding to remaining categorical strings. Our target variable was defined as a binary indicator for Household Income $\ge$ $50,000. Essentially this just means I dropped any non-numeric values so the model could be trained appropriately, and then told it what to predict with the data it recieves._

```{python}
# 1. Create the Target Column
sw_ml_ready = sw_ml.with_columns(
  target = pl.when(pl.col("income_num") >= 3).then(1).otherwise(0)
)

# 2. Drop identifiers and original target source
cols_to_drop = ["RespondentID", "first_name", "last_name", "email", "name_part", "income_num"]
sw_ml_ready = sw_ml_ready.drop(cols_to_drop)

# 3. One-Hot Encode
sw_final = sw_ml_ready.to_dummies(
  columns=[col for col, dtype in sw_ml_ready.schema.items() if dtype == pl.String]
)

# 4. Handle any remaining Nulls
sw_final = sw_final.fill_null(0)

print(f"Final feature count: {len(sw_final.columns)}")
```

## Results

### Model Description
_I utilized a Random Forest Classifier with 100 estimators. This model is ideal for survey data as it captures interactions between features (for example, how Age and Education combined might predict Income) without assuming a linear relationship._

### Model Accuracy
_The model achieved an accuracy of approximately 59.88%. This model successfully exceeds the client's 55% accuracy threshold. It may seem like a lower score than what is generally hoped for, but when you think about how accurately it is able to predict a person's yearly income based off of their opinions on Star Wars films, it seems like a reasonable, and even impressive performance._

```{python}
# Separate Features
X = sw_final.drop("target")
y = sw_final.select("target")

# Split data
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
  X.to_numpy(), 
  y.to_numpy().ravel(), 
  test_size=0.2, 
  random_state=42
)
# Initialize and Train
model = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Predict and Evaluate
y_pred = model.predict(X_test)
accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)

print(f"Model Accuracy: {accuracy:.2%}")
print("\nClassification Report:")
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred))
```

### Feature Importance
_The following chart shows which factors were the strongest predictors of a respondent making over $50k. Higher scores indicate greater importance in the model's decision-making process._

```{python}
feature_importance_df = pl.DataFrame({
    "feature": X.columns,
    "importance": model.feature_importances_
}).sort("importance", descending=True).head(10)

p_importance = (
  ggplot(feature_importance_df, aes(x=as_discrete('feature', order_by='importance'), y='importance')) +
  geom_bar(stat='identity', fill='#008fd5') +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title="Top 10 Predictors of Income >= $50k",
    subtitle="Key factors identifying high-income respondents",
    x="", 
    y="Importance Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
)

p_importance.show()
```

## Data Validation - Stretch Challenge

_To ensure the cleaning didn't compromise the data integrity, I recreated two visuals from the FiveThirtyEight article. This shows that the data matches the original publication. Matching these values also confirms the dataset is ready for predictive modeling._

```{python}
seen_cols = [f"seen_episode_{i}" for i in ["I", "II", "III", "IV", "V", "VI"]]
movie_labels = ["The Phantom Menace", "Attack of the Clones", "Revenge of the Sith", "A New Hope", "The Empire Strikes Back", "Return of the Jedi"]

seen_data = (
    sw_ml.select(seen_cols)
    .select([(pl.col(c).is_not_null().sum() / 835 * 100).alias(c) for c in seen_cols])
    .unpivot(variable_name="movie", value_name="pct")
    .with_columns(
        movie_title = pl.col("movie").replace(dict(zip(seen_cols, movie_labels)))
    )
)

shot_first = (
    sw_ml.group_by("who_shot_first")
    .len()
    .filter(pl.col("who_shot_first").is_not_null())
    .with_columns(pct = pl.col("len") / pl.col("len").sum() * 100)
)
```

```{python}
seen_data = seen_data.with_columns(
  pct_label = (pl.col("pct").round(0).cast(pl.Int64).cast(pl.Utf8))
)

final_movie_order = [
  "Return of the Jedi", 
  "The Empire Strikes Back", 
  "A New Hope", 
  "Revenge of the Sith", 
  "Attack of the Clones", 
  "The Phantom Menace"
]

p1 = (ggplot(seen_data, aes(x='movie_title', y='pct')) +
  geom_bar(stat='identity', fill='#008fd5', width=0.45) +
  geom_text(aes(label='pct_label'), nudge_y=3.5, va="bottom", size=7) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_x_discrete(limits=final_movie_order) + 
  scale_y_continuous(limits=[0, 105]) +
  labs(title="Which 'Star Wars' Movies Have You Seen?", subtitle="Of 835 respondents who have seen any film",x="", y="") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis_text_y = element_blank(),
    axis_ticks_y = element_blank(),
    panel_grid_major = element_blank(),
    panel_grid_minor = element_blank(),
    axis_text_x = element_text(size=14, color='#333333'),
    plot_title = element_text(face='bold', size=15),
    plot_subtitle = element_text(size=14, color='#555555'),
    panel_spacing_y = 0.05,
  ))
p1.show()
```

```{python}
shot_first = shot_first.with_columns(
  pct_label = (pl.col("pct").round(0).cast(pl.Int64).cast(pl.Utf8))
)

final_shot_order = [
  "I don't understand this question",
  "Greedo",
  "Han"
]

p2 = (ggplot(shot_first, aes(x='who_shot_first', y='pct')) +
  geom_bar(stat='identity', fill='#008fd5', width=0.40) +
  geom_text(aes(label='pct_label'), nudge_y=3, hjust=0, size=7) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_x_discrete(limits=final_shot_order) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits=[0, 55]) +
  labs(title="Who Shot First?",subtitle="According to 835 Star Wars respondents", x="", y="") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis_text_y = element_blank(),
    axis_ticks_y = element_blank(),
    panel_grid_major = element_blank(),
    panel_grid_minor = element_blank(),
    axis_text_x = element_text(size=14, color='#333333'),
    plot_title = element_text(face='bold', size=15.5),
    plot_subtitle = element_text(size=14, color='#555555'),
    panel_spacing_y = 0.05,
  ))

p2.show()
```
